The disciplined developer stops at a breakpoint with a specific question — 'is this value what I expect here?' — reads exactly the state needed to answer it, and uses the answer to confirm or refute a theory about the cause, then moves the investigation forward. Aimlessly staring at all the variables is far less effective than inspecting the few that a hypothesis predicts should be wrong.

Thus, inspecting run-time state through the locals, watch, call-stack, expression-evaluation and threads facilities of a debugger turns a program's hidden internals into concrete, examinable data; by reading the values a hypothesis predicts, walking the call stack to see how they arose, and even modifying values to test 'what if', the developer converts guesswork about a defect into direct, evidence-based understanding.
questions: What is the difference between the locals/variables window and the watch window?
What does the call stack show, and why is being able to select a different frame important?
How can evaluating expressions and modifying variables during a pause help test a hypothesis, and what is the associated risk?
How does a debugger help inspect complex data such as objects, collections and pointers?
Why is a threads window necessary when debugging concurrent programs?

title: Using debugger commands: continue, stop, step forward
outline: Execution-control commands as the way to drive a paused program.
Continue (resume) and run-to-cursor.
Step over, step into and step out.
Stopping, pausing, restarting and detaching.
Advanced control: set-next-statement and reverse execution.
body: Once a debugger has paused a program, execution-control commands are how the developer drives it forward in a controlled way, deciding how much code runs before the next pause. Mastering this small set of commands — continue, the several kinds of step, and stop — is what turns a static pause into an active investigation of how the program behaves statement by statement.

The 'continue' (or 'resume') command tells the program to run at full speed until it either hits the next breakpoint, throws an exception, or terminates. It is used after the developer has finished inspecting one pause point and wants to reach the next area of interest without walking through the intervening code. In command-line debuggers it is typically 'c' or 'continue'; in IDEs it is a play button, often bound to F5.

A close relative is 'run to cursor', which continues execution until it reaches the line where the text cursor is currently placed, effectively setting a one-shot temporary breakpoint. This saves the trouble of creating and later removing a breakpoint when the developer just wants to skip ahead to a specific spot once.

The stepping commands are the heart of fine-grained control, and the distinction among them is essential. 'Step over' executes the current line completely and pauses on the next line in the same function; if the current line contains a function call, that entire call runs without pausing inside it. Step over is the right choice when the developer trusts a called function and only wants to follow the flow of the current routine. It is usually bound to F10.

'Step into' also executes the current line, but if that line contains a function call, the debugger pauses at the first line inside the called function, letting the developer descend into it and examine its execution. Step into is used when the suspect logic may lie within the called function. It is typically bound to F11. Choosing correctly between step over and step into at each call is what keeps a debugging session focused rather than wandering into irrelevant library code.

'Step out' (or 'finish') runs the rest of the current function at full speed and pauses as soon as it returns to its caller, at the line following the call. It is the complement of step into: when a developer has stepped into a function and confirmed that the interesting part is elsewhere, step out escapes back to the calling context without single-stepping through the remainder. It is often bound to Shift+F11.

Stopping the debugging session is done with a 'stop' or 'terminate' command, which ends the program's execution entirely and closes the debugging session. This is distinct from 'detach', which disconnects the debugger but leaves the process running — important when debugging a live server that should not be killed. Understanding the difference prevents accidentally shutting down a production process one only meant to observe.

Several debuggers also offer a 'pause' (or 'break all') command that interrupts a program that is currently running, as opposed to one already stopped at a breakpoint. This is how a developer regains control of a program stuck in an apparent infinite loop or a hang: pausing it reveals, through the call stack, exactly where it is spending its time, which often immediately identifies the offending loop or blocking call.

A 'restart' command stops the current session and launches it again from the beginning in one action, which is convenient when the developer realises a breakpoint should have been set earlier or wants to retry with adjustments. In command-line debuggers this corresponds to the 'run' command issued again, sometimes with different arguments.

More advanced control commands give still finer power. 'Set next statement' (move the execution pointer) lets the developer change which line will execute next within the current function — skipping over code or re-running a block — without actually executing the code in between. This is useful for testing but dangerous, because it can leave the program in an inconsistent state, so it must be used with a clear understanding of what is being bypassed.

Reverse or time-travel debugging extends the model backwards. In debuggers that support it, such as GDB in record mode, rr, or Visual Studio's IntelliTrace and snapshot features, commands like 'step back' and 'reverse-continue' undo execution to a previous state. This is extraordinarily helpful when a developer steps one line too far past the moment a value went wrong, since normally the only recourse would be to restart and try again.

These commands are almost always available both as IDE buttons with standard function-key shortcuts and as typed commands in command-line debuggers, and their meanings are consistent across tools: continue, step over, step into, step out and stop behave the same in Visual Studio, GDB, LLDB and pdb. Learning the concepts once therefore transfers directly to any debugger, and learning the keyboard shortcuts makes stepping fast enough to keep pace with one's reasoning.

Thus, the debugger's execution-control commands — continue and run-to-cursor to move between regions, step over, step into and step out to navigate calls at the right granularity, pause to seize a running program, and stop or detach to end a session — are the vocabulary with which a developer drives a paused program; used deliberately, and augmented where available by set-next-statement and reverse execution, they let the developer follow a defect to its exact origin one controlled move at a time.
questions: What does the 'continue' command do, and how does 'run to cursor' relate to it?
What is the difference between 'step over', 'step into' and 'step out', and when is each appropriate?
How does stopping a debugging session differ from detaching from the process, and why does the distinction matter?
How can the 'pause' (break all) command help diagnose a program stuck in an infinite loop?
What does 'set next statement' do, why is it risky, and what advantage does reverse (time-travel) debugging provide?
